title: Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time
subtitle: Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.
caption:
Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time

Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.

The stacked area chart tracks the cumulative count of discovered Near-Earth asteroids from 1990 through 2025, categorized by physical diameter. The vertical axis measures the total number of objects, reaching a combined total of 40,000 by the final year shown. The data is divided into three distinct size classes: asteroids larger than 1 kilometer, those between 140 meters and 1 kilometer, and those smaller than 140 meters. Near-Earth asteroids are defined as those whose closest approach to the Sun is less than 1.3 astronomical units. The bottom layer, representing the largest asteroids exceeding 1 kilometer in diameter, shows a slow, steady increase that flattens out near the top of the timeline, indicating that the majority of these massive objects have already been identified. The middle layer, covering sizes between 140 meters and 1 kilometer, displays a consistent upward trajectory, contributing significantly to the total count. The top and largest section of the chart, representing asteroids smaller than 140 meters, drives the overall exponential growth curve. This category begins near zero in 1990 and expands rapidly after 2010, accounting for the bulk of the discoveries in the most recent decade. By 2025, the cumulative total reaches 40,000, with the smallest size class comprising the vast majority of the recorded population. The visual pattern shows a sharp acceleration in detection rates for smaller objects in the 21st century compared to the relatively stable discovery rate for the largest kilometer-scale bodies.
